Food.example_norsk: Jeg spiser ferskt brød til frokost.
Food.example: I eat fresh bread for breakfast.
Food.fun_fact: Bread is a staple of the Norwegian diet, often served as 'matpakke'.

Food.example_norsk: Kan jeg få et glass vann?
Food.example: Can I have a glass of water?
Food.fun_fact: Norway has some of the cleanest tap water in the world.

Food.example: Norwegians drink a lot of coffee.
Food.fun_fact: Norway is one of the top coffee-consuming nations per capita.

Food.example: The child is drinking a glass of milk.
Food.fun_fact: Tine is the largest dairy cooperative in Norway.

Food.example: The apple is red and sweet.
Food.fun_fact: Hardanger is famous for its delicious Norwegian apples.

Animals.example: The dog is running in the garden.
Animals.fun_fact: The Lundehund is a unique Norwegian breed with six toes on each foot.

Animals.example: The cat is sleeping on the sofa.
Animals.fun_fact: The Norwegian Forest Cat is known for its thick, water-repellent fur.

Animals.example: The moose is the king of the forest.
Animals.fun_fact: There are over 100,000 moose in Norway.

Animals.example: The bird is singing in the tree.
Animals.fun_fact: The White-throated Dipper (Fossekall) is Norway's national bird.
Brown bears can still be found in remote parts of Norway.

Work.example_norsk: Barna går til skolen hver morgen.
Work.example: The children go to school every morning.
Work.fun_fact: School is mandatory in Norway from ages 6 to 16.

Work.example_norsk: Jeg trives veldig godt i jobben min.
Work.example: I enjoy my job very much.
Work.fun_fact: Norway has a strong 'flat hierarchy' at work, meaning bosses and employees are quite equal.
Teachers in Norway focus a lot on outdoor learning and 'friluftsliv'.

Work.example_norsk: Jeg må spare penger til ferien.
Work.example: I need to save money for the vacation.
Work.fun_fact: The Norwegian currency is the 'Krone', and Norway is almost a cashless society.
In Norway, the first day of school is a huge celebration for first-graders.

City.example: The shop closes at eight o'clock.
City.fun_fact: Most grocery stores in Norway are closed on Sundays.
Karl Johans gate is the main street in Oslo.
Norway is famous for its 'Stave Churches' (stavkirke) made of wood.

City.example: The hospital is located in the city center.
City.fun_fact: Healthcare in Norwegian hospitals is free for all residents.

City.example_norsk: Vi skal spise på restaurant i kveld.
City.example: We are going to eat at a restaurant tonight.
City.fun_fact: Tipping is appreciated in Norway but not mandatory like in the US.

Colors.fun_fact: Licorice (lakris) is a very popular black candy in Norway.
The word 'gråvær' (grey weather) is common for overcast days.
Brunost (brown cheese) is an iconic Norwegian food made from whey.
Oranges are a traditional snack during Easter skiing trips.

Colors.example: The flower is beautiful and purple.
Colors.fun_fact: Purple is the traditional color of Advent in Norway.

Verbs.example_norsk: Jeg liker å løpe i skogen.
Verbs.example: I like to run in the forest.
Verbs.fun_fact: Norway holds many world records in middle-distance running.

Verbs.example: He reads the newspaper every morning.
Verbs.fun_fact: 'Påskekrim' is a tradition where Norwegians read crime novels during Easter.

Verbs.example_norsk: Jeg skriver et brev til bestemor.
Verbs.example: I am writing a letter to grandmother.
Verbs.fun_fact: The Norwegian alphabet has three extra letters: Æ, Ø, and Å.
There are hundreds of different dialects in Norway.
Babies in Norway often nap outside in their strollers, even in winter!

Furniture.example: The food is on the table.
Furniture.fun_fact: Scandinavian design often uses light-colored wood for tables.
Peter Opsvik is a famous Norwegian designer who made the Tripp Trapp chair.

Furniture.example: We are relaxing on the sofa.
Furniture.fun_fact: The word 'koselig' often involves sitting on a sofa with a blanket.

Furniture.example: The books are on the shelf.
Furniture.fun_fact: Modular shelving units are a staple of Norwegian interior design.

Furniture.example: The lamp gives off a warm light.
Furniture.fun_fact: Lighting is very important in Norway due to the dark winter months.

Kitchen_Tools.example_norsk: Jeg spiser salat med en gaffel.
Kitchen_Tools.example: I eat salad with a fork.
Kitchen_Tools.fun_fact: Traditional Norwegian silver forks often have intricate patterns.

Kitchen_Tools.example_norsk: Kan jeg få en skje til suppen?
Kitchen_Tools.example: Can I have a spoon for the soup?
Kitchen_Tools.fun_fact: Wooden spoons are common for stirring 'grøt' (porridge).

Kitchen_Tools.example: Put the plate in the dishwasher.
Kitchen_Tools.fun_fact: A 'flatbrød' is a traditional crispy bread served on a side plate.

Kitchen_Tools.example_norsk: Vil du ha en kopp kaffe?
Kitchen_Tools.example: Do you want a cup of coffee?
Kitchen_Tools.fun_fact: Norwegians love 'turkopp', a wooden cup used for hiking.

Kitchen_Tools.example: The glass is full of juice.
Kitchen_Tools.fun_fact: Hadeland Glassverk is one of Norway's oldest industrial companies.

Geography.fun_fact: Norway shares borders with Sweden, Finland, and Russia.

Geography.example_norsk: Oslo er den største byen i Norge.
Geography.example: Oslo is the largest city in Norway.
Geography.fun_fact: Bergen and Trondheim were both capitals of Norway before Oslo.

Geography.example: Oslo is the capital of Norway.
Geography.fun_fact: The Royal Palace is located at the end of the main street in the capital.

Geography.example_norsk: Jeg vil reise rundt i verden.
Geography.example: I want to travel around the world.
Geography.fun_fact: Norway is one of the northernmost countries in the world.

Geography.example: We have to look at the map.
Geography.fun_fact: Paper maps are still popular for hiking in the Norwegian mountains.

Post_Office.example_norsk: Jeg fikk et brev i posten.
Post_Office.example: I received a letter in the mail.

Post_Office.fun_fact: Most Norwegians pick up packages at 'Post i Butikk' (post inside a grocery store).

Post_Office.example: Remember to put on a stamp.
Post_Office.fun_fact: The first Norwegian postage stamp was issued in 1855.
Norwegian addresses always include a four-digit postal code.
Green is the standard color for private mailboxes in Norway.

In_The_Garden.fun_fact: Heather (røsslyng) is the national flower of Norway.
The 'Old Tjikko' spruce is one of the oldest trees in the world (found in Scandinavia).

In_The_Garden.example: The grass needs to be mowed.
In_The_Garden.fun_fact: Some traditional Norwegian houses have grass growing on the roof (torvtak).

In_The_Garden.example: We are sitting outside in the garden.
In_The_Garden.fun_fact: Having a garden is a very common way for Norwegians to enjoy the summer.

In_The_Garden.example_norsk: Det er en stor stein på stien.
In_The_Garden.example: There is a large stone on the path.
In_The_Garden.fun_fact: Norway's mountains are mostly made of ancient granite and gneiss.

Shopping.fun_fact: Most shops now offer digital receipts through banking apps.

Shopping.example: What is the price of this one?
Shopping.fun_fact: Norway uses the 'krone' (NOK) as its currency.
Plastic bags in Norway now cost a few kroner to encourage recycling.

Shopping.example_norsk: Senteret er åpent til klokken ni.
Shopping.example: The mall is open until nine o'clock.
Shopping.fun_fact: Norway has some of the highest mall density in Europe.

Shopping.example: There is a sale in the clothing store.
Shopping.fun_fact: January sales (januarsalg) are a major event in Norway.

Hygiene.fun_fact: Traditional Norwegian soap was often made from pine oil.
In Norway, towels are often dried on heated towel rails.

Hygiene.example: I look at myself in the mirror.
Hygiene.fun_fact: Round mirrors are currently very popular in Scandinavian interior design.

Hygiene.example_norsk: Jeg grer håret med en kam.
Hygiene.example: I am combing my hair with a comb.
Hygiene.fun_fact: Vikings were known for carrying beautifully carved combs made of bone.

Hygiene.example: We are out of toilet paper.
Hygiene.fun_fact: Norway produces a large amount of its own recycled paper products.

Ocean_Life.example: The salmon is swimming in the river.
Ocean_Life.fun_fact: Norway is the world's leading producer of Atlantic salmon.

Ocean_Life.example: We are eating shrimp on the pier.
Ocean_Life.fun_fact: Fresh shrimp with white bread and lemon is a classic Norwegian summer meal.

Ocean_Life.example_norsk: Vi så en hval i fjorden.
Ocean_Life.example: We saw a whale in the fjord.
Ocean_Life.fun_fact: Norway is one of the few countries that still practices traditional whaling.

Ocean_Life.example: The crab has a hard shell.
Ocean_Life.fun_fact: The King Crab is an invasive but valuable species in Northern Norway.

Ocean_Life.example: The seal is lying on the ice.
Ocean_Life.fun_fact: The harbor seal is a common sight along the Norwegian coast.

Materials.example: The house is built of wood.
Materials.fun_fact: Norway has a long tradition of expert woodworking and carpentry.

Materials.example: The key is made of metal.
Materials.fun_fact: Hydro is a major Norwegian company that produces aluminum.

Materials.example: The bottle is made of plastic.
Materials.fun_fact: Norway has a very high recycling rate for plastic bottles (the 'Pant' system).

Materials.example_norsk: Ull holder deg varm om vinteren.
Materials.example: Wool keeps you warm in the winter.
Materials.fun_fact: Norwegian wool is famous for being durable and warm.

Materials.example: The window is made of glass.
Materials.fun_fact: Double or triple glazing is standard in Norway to keep the heat in.

Emergency.fun_fact: Most Norwegian police officers do not carry firearms on their person daily.
The emergency number for medical help in Norway is 113.

Emergency.example: The fire truck is driving with sirens.
Emergency.fun_fact: The emergency number for fire in Norway is 110.
The word 'dugnad' is a unique Norwegian word for helping each other.

Emergency.example: There is a fire in the forest.
Emergency.fun_fact: Many historic Norwegian towns were destroyed by fire due to wooden buildings.

Technology.fun_fact: Norway was the first non-English speaking country to connect to the internet (ARPANET).
Norwegians spend an average of 4-6 hours on screens per day.
BankID is a secure password system used by almost everyone in Norway.
Email is still the most common way to communicate formally in Norway.

Technology.example_norsk: Har du en lader til telefonen?
Technology.example: Do you have a charger for the phone?
Technology.fun_fact: Universal USB-C chargers are now the standard in Norway.

Weather_Extra.example_norsk: Jeg kan ikke se veien på grunn av tåke.
Weather_Extra.example: I cannot see the road because of fog.
Weather_Extra.fun_fact: Fog is very common in the fjords during autumn.
Thunderstorms are less frequent in Norway than in southern Europe.

Weather_Extra.example_norsk: Det er meldt storm på kysten.
Weather_Extra.example: A storm is predicted on the coast.
Weather_Extra.fun_fact: Norwegians use the app 'Yr' to check for storms.
Talking about the weather is the #1 icebreaker in Norway.
Dry cold is often felt as more comfortable than wet cold.

Sports.fun_fact: Norway's Erling Haaland is one of the best players in the world.
The Norwegian women's handball team is world-famous.

Sports.example_norsk: Vi går på skøyter på vannet.
Sports.example: We are skating on the lake.
Sports.fun_fact: Speed skating is a historic national sport in Norway.
Community spirit in sports is very strong in Norway.

Music_Instruments.example: He plays the guitar by the campfire.
Music_Instruments.fun_fact: The guitar is the most popular instrument for beginners in Norway.

Music_Instruments.example: She plays beautifully on the piano.
Music_Instruments.fun_fact: Edvard Grieg's piano concerto is world-renowned.

Music_Instruments.example_norsk: Ikke slå så hardt på tromma.
Music_Instruments.example: Don't hit the drum so hard.
Music_Instruments.fun_fact: Drums have been used in Sami culture for centuries.
The Hardanger Fiddle has 8 or 9 strings instead of 4.

Music_Instruments.example: The flute has a bright sound.
Music_Instruments.fun_fact: Willow flutes (seljefløyte) are traditional Norwegian folk instruments.

Buildings_Parts.example: The lamp hangs from the ceiling.
Buildings_Parts.fun_fact: Norwegian roofs are built to withstand heavy snow loads.
Heated floors (varmekabler) are a standard luxury in Norway.

Buildings_Parts.example: The picture is hanging on the wall.
Buildings_Parts.fun_fact: Wooden paneling (panel) is very common on Norwegian walls.

Buildings_Parts.example_norsk: Gå opp trappen til andre etasje.
Buildings_Parts.example: Go up the stairs to the second floor.
Buildings_Parts.fun_fact: Norway has the world's longest wooden staircase in Flørli (4444 steps).

Buildings_Parts.example: It is dark in the basement.
Buildings_Parts.fun_fact: Basements are often used as 'hobbyrom' (hobby rooms) in Norway.

Nature_Objects.example: The leaves are falling off the trees.
Nature_Objects.fun_fact: The colors of the leaves in the mountains (høstfarger) are stunning.

Nature_Objects.example: The bird is sitting on a branch.
Nature_Objects.fun_fact: Spruce branches were traditionally used as doormats.

Nature_Objects.example: The children are playing in the sand.
Nature_Objects.fun_fact: Norway has several 'arctic beaches' with coral-white sand.

Nature_Objects.example: The path is full of mud.
Nature_Objects.fun_fact: Springtime in Norway is often called 'gjørmetid' (mud time).

Nature_Objects.example_norsk: Pass deg, det er glatt på isen.
Nature_Objects.example: Watch out, it's slippery on the ice.
Nature_Objects.fun_fact: Glaciers in Norway are thousands of years old.

Feelings_Extra.fun_fact: Norwegians value politeness and rarely show anger in public.
There are very few dangerous animals in the Norwegian wild.
Norwegians are very proud of their local dialects.
The word 'stusselig' is a unique way to describe something slightly sad or pathetic.

Bedroom.example: The duvet is good and warm.
Bedroom.fun_fact: Norwegians use down-filled duvets even in the summer.
Pillow fights are called 'putekrig' in Norwegian.

Bedroom.example: The alarm clock rings at seven o'clock.
Bedroom.fun_fact: Most Norwegians now use their smartphones as alarm clocks.

Bedroom.example: The clothes are hanging in the closet.
Bedroom.fun_fact: Built-in closets are standard in most Norwegian bedrooms.

Bedroom.example: The book is on the nightstand.
Bedroom.fun_fact: A nightstand is literally a 'night table' in Norwegian.

Sky_Space.example: The moon lights up the night.
Sky_Space.fun_fact: The full moon over the snowy mountains is a popular photo motif.
Because there is little light pollution, stars are very visible in rural Norway.

Sky_Space.example_norsk: Det er ikke en sky på himmelen.
Sky_Space.example: There isn't a cloud in the sky.
Sky_Space.fun_fact: The Norwegian word for 'cloud' is the same as the word for 'sky' in English (context matters!).
The Northern Lights (Nordlys) appear high in the Norwegian sky.
Norway has a space center on the island of Andøya.

Beach_Holiday.example: We are playing with a beach ball.
Beach_Holiday.fun_fact: Beach balls are a staple of Norwegian summer vacations.
Even in the mountains, the sun can be very strong due to snow reflection.

Beach_Holiday.example: Lay the towel on the sand.
Beach_Holiday.fun_fact: Microfiber towels are becoming popular for hikers.
Norwegian children often collect blue mussel shells (blåskjell).
Stad is the most wave-heavy place in Norway, popular for surfing.

Winter_Clothing.example: Wool mittens are best in the cold.
Winter_Clothing.fun_fact: Selbuvotter are the most famous patterned mittens from Norway.

Winter_Clothing.example: Wrap the scarf around your neck.
Winter_Clothing.fun_fact: Long, thick wool scarves are a fashion staple in Oslo.

Winter_Clothing.example_norsk: Hun har på seg en fin kåpe.
Winter_Clothing.example: She is wearing a nice coat.
Winter_Clothing.fun_fact: The word 'kåpe' usually refers to a dressier, long coat.
Rubber boots (gummistøvler) are essential for rainy days.

Winter_Clothing.example: We have cocoa in the thermos.
Winter_Clothing.fun_fact: No Norwegian 'skitur' (ski trip) is complete without a thermos.
